reason: Con argument cites cybersecurity vulnerabilities that could cause accidents, challenging the safety premise of pro1.
The weather‑performance issue challenges the premise that autonomous vehicles can safely reduce accidents
Potential sensor failures undermine the premise that autonomous vehicles will lower accident rates
Cybersecurity vulnerabilities could prevent the reliable vehicle‑spacing optimization that underlies the capacity claim.
Poor performance in unpredictable weather could disrupt the precise spacing needed for increased capacity.
High development costs challenge the premise that many autonomous cars will be deployed to increase capacity
Potential sensor failures undermine the premise that autonomous cars can safely optimize spacing
Con1 challenges the safety premise needed for AVs to reliably provide mobility to non‑drivers.
Con3 questions the ability of AVs to operate safely, undermining the premise that they can reliably serve non‑drivers.
Con4 raises liability issues that could deter adoption, weakening the premise that AVs will provide mobility.
Con5 points out high costs, undermining the premise that AVs will be accessible to those who cannot drive.
The claim that sensor failures could occur challenges the premise that autonomous vehicles reliably provide mobility for non‑drivers.
The inequality argument questions the premise that autonomous vehicles will broadly increase mobility for those who cannot drive.
Con1 questions the safety premise underlying Pro5, suggesting cybersecurity risks could prevent passengers from freely using travel time
Con2 challenges the societal premise that autonomous vehicles will be accepted enough for passengers to rely on them for work or leisure
Con3 attacks the reliability premise of Pro5 by highlighting weather‑related safety limitations
Con4 undermines the trust premise of Pro5 by pointing out complex liability issues that could deter use
Con5 challenges the economic premise that autonomous vehicles will be widely accessible for passengers to benefit from productive travel time
The claim that sensors may miss edge cases attacks the safety premise that enables passengers to work during travel.
The inequality argument challenges the premise that autonomous vehicles will be widely accessible for all passengers.
Cybersecurity vulnerabilities challenge the premise that reducing private cars via fleets is an unqualified benefit.
The claim that autonomous systems struggle in bad weather attacks the premise that fleets will be reliable enough to reduce private car ownership
Complex liability after accidents challenges the feasibility premise underlying the reduction‑of‑cars argument
High development costs undermine the premise that autonomous fleets will be affordable enough to replace private cars
Even if fleets cut private car numbers, they may encourage urban sprawl, showing the conclusion does not guarantee the intended benefit
Potential erosion of human driving skills attacks the societal‑benefit premise of the private‑car‑reduction claim
Privacy concerns undermine the acceptance premise needed for autonomous fleets to reduce private car ownership
Sensor failures in edge cases challenge the safety premise that makes fleet adoption—and thus car‑number reduction—plausible
Socio‑economic inequality concerns attack the premise that fleet benefits will be broadly accessible, weakening the reduction claim
Cybersecurity vulnerabilities threaten the safe integration of self‑driving cars with smart‑city systems.
Unreliable performance in bad weather challenges the premise that integration will reliably smooth traffic.
Complex liability issues could impede deployment of integrated autonomous systems.
High development costs may limit widespread adoption needed for traffic‑flow benefits.
Even if integration works, induced urban sprawl could generate additional traffic, negating the claimed smoothing effect.
Erosion of human driving skills raises doubts about the reliability of a system that depends on human oversight.
Privacy concerns over data sharing can block the cooperation needed for smart‑city integration.
Con9’s claim that sensors may miss rare edge‑cases challenges the premise that integration with smart‑city infrastructure will reliably smooth traffic flow.
Con1’s cybersecurity vulnerability claim attacks the premise that autonomous vehicles can operate continuously without interruption.
Con3’s assertion that AVs struggle in unpredictable weather undermines the premise of uninterrupted, fatigue‑free operation.
The cybersecurity vulnerability claim challenges the premise that self‑driving tech will be widely adopted to boost EV uptake.
Weather‑related safety issues question the premise that self‑driving tech can reliably drive more EVs.
High development costs challenge the premise that self‑driving technology will be affordable enough to spur EV adoption.
The cybersecurity vulnerability claim attacks the premise that data collection will be beneficial for planning.
Weather‑related operational limits challenge the premise that sufficient data will be gathered for planning.
Con8 challenges the premise that data collection is beneficial by raising privacy concerns.
Con9 questions the reliability of the data by pointing to sensor failures, undermining the pro's premise.
B's claim that sensor technology may fail challenges the premise that autonomous cars will be widely deployed, which A relies on to argue about massive driver job losses.
High development costs may limit adoption, weakening the premise that AVs will make long commutes easier.
If sensor technology cannot handle edge‑case scenarios, AVs may not become common enough to influence commuting patterns.
Limited access to AVs for only affluent users challenges the premise that they will broadly enable longer commutes.
